Offiah in 2016]] '''Martin Nwokocha Offiah''' [[Order of the British Empire|MBE]] ({{IPAc-en|ɒ|ˈ|f|i|ə}} {{Respell|O|fee|ə}} (''original pronunciation''), {{IPAc-en|ə|ˈ|f|aɪ|ə}} {{Respell|ə|FYE|ə}} (''commonly used'')); born 29 December 1965){{efn|name=DOB}} is an [[England|English]] former professional [[rugby league]] and [[rugby union]] footballer who played in the 1980s, 1990s and 2000s.{{cite web |title=An Audience with Martin Offiah – The Old Courts |url=https://www.theoldcourts.com/shows/an-audience-with-martin-offiah/ |access-date=1 March 2022 |website=theoldcourts.com}} He scored over 500 tries during his rugby league career, making him the third-highest try scorer (and top try-scoring English player) of all time.{{cite web |last=Heppenstall |first=Ross |date=2021-10-29 |title=Martin Offiah remembers sickening racial abuse endured during his career |url=https://www.manchestereveningnews.co.uk/sport/rugby-league/martin-offiah-racial-abuse-rugby-22005241 |access-date=1 March 2022 |website=YorkshireLive}} Offiah was inducted into the [[Rugby Football League Hall of Fame|Rugby League Hall of Fame]] in 2013, and now features in a statue of great rugby league players outside Wembley Stadium.{{cite web|last=Wilson|first=Andy|title=Offiah bows out with grace|url=https://www.theguardian.com/sport/2001/sep/15/rugbyleague.andywilson|work=The Guardian|location=London|access-date=21 December 2013|date=15 September 2001}} He was a Great Britain and England international [[Rugby league positions|winger]], and due to his running speed he was nicknamed "Chariots" Offiah after the film ''[[Chariots of Fire]]''.{{cite news |title=Why is Swing Low, Sweet Chariot the England rugby song? |url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/news/uk-england-51646140 |publisher=[[BBC News]] |date=19 June 2020}} Offiah played rugby union before moving to English rugby league club [[Widnes Vikings|Widnes]] in 1987. He later played for [[Wigan Warriors|Wigan]], [[London Broncos#1994–2005: Broncos and Super League|London Broncos]] and [[Salford Red Devils|Salford City Reds]], and in Australia for [[Sydney Roosters|Eastern Suburbs]] and [[St. George Dragons|St. George]]. After rugby union turned professional, Offiah also had short spells playing for [[Bedford Blues|Bedford]] and [[Wasps RFC|London Wasps]]. Since retiring Offiah has appeared on several reality TV shows.{{cite news| url=https://www.independent.co.uk/sport/general/rugby-league/offiah-seals-deal-for-recordbreaking-teen-1783432.html| work=The Independent| location=London| title=Offiah seals deal for record-breaking teen| first=Dave| last=Hadfield| date=8 September 2009| access-date=12 May 2010}} In May 2023, he was appointed an ambassador of Wigan Warriors. ==Early life== Offiah was born in Hackney, London, England{{cite web |title=Martin Offiah |url=https://www.imdb.com/name/nm1567280/bio |access-date=1 March 2022 |publisher=IMDb}} to Nigerian parents of [[Igbo people|Igbo]] origin, and attended [[Woolverstone Hall School]] near Ipswich, Suffolk, where he represented the school at fencing and played rugby union and cricket. He played cricket for the Essex 2nd XI but soon realised his main talent was for rugby union. He first played club-level [[rugby union]] for [[Ipswich RFC]] and [[Rosslyn Park F.C.|Rosslyn Park]]. After starring on the [[rugby sevens]] circuit and for the [[Barbarian F.C.|Barbarians]], there was talk of an England future for Offiah.{{cite news |title=Gareth Thomas completes switch from union to Wales rugby league side Crusaders |url=https://www.telegraph.co.uk/sport/rugbyunion/international/wales/7376424/Gareth-Thomas-completes-switch-from-union-to-Wales-rugby-league-side-Crusaders.html |work=The Daily Telegraph|location=London |date=5 March 2010 |access-date=2 May 2010}} ==Professional playing career== ===1987–91: Widnes=== '''Inc. 1989 summer stint at Eastern Suburbs & 1991 summer stint at St. George Dragons''' Offiah was spotted by Widnes coach [[Doug Laughton]] playing in the [[Middlesex Sevens]], and was signed by the club to play rugby league for the [[1987–88 Rugby Football League season|1987–88 season]]. [[Alex Murphy (rugby league)|Alex Murphy]] had been interested in acquiring Offiah from rugby union, but the board of directors at St. Helens infamously referred to Offiah as an "uncoordinated clown" and the interest was not followed up with action. Within months, he became the most talked-about winger since [[Billy Boston]] for his lightning speed and try-scoring; at Widnes he forged a reputation as a devastating winger, helping the team win both the [[Rugby Football League Championship|Championship]] and [[Rugby League Premiership|Premiership]]. He ended his début season with 42 tries,{{cite news|title=Overjoyed Offiah drops quit hint|url=https://news.bbc.co.uk/sport2/hi/rugby_league/1417203.stm|publisher=BBC Sport|access-date=6 August 2014|date=1 July 2001}} making him the league's top try scorer, also breaking the club record held by [[Frank Myler]] for most tries scored in a season.{{cite web|title=Martin Offiah MBE|url=http://www.therfl.co.uk/the-rfl/history_and_heritage/rugby_league_hall_of_fame/martin_offiah|publisher=The Rugby Football League|access-date=6 August 2014}} He received the [[Man of Steel Award|Man of Steel award]] for his efforts. He was also rewarded with a place in the [[1988 Great Britain Lions tour of Australasia]], where he played in all three Tests of the [[Ashes series (rugby league)|Ashes series]] which was lost to Australia. While on tour with the Lions, Offiah took part in a 100-metre match race at [[Wentworth Park]] in [[Sydney]] against Australian flyers [[Dale Shearer]] and [[John Ferguson (rugby league)|John Ferguson]] (Ferguson replaced [[Greg Alexander]] who was forced to withdraw through injury). Offiah easily won the race from Shearer and Ferguson.{{cn|date=October 2025}} In the [[1988–89 Rugby Football League season|1988–89 season]], Widnes won the Championship and Premiership for the second successive year, and Offiah was again the league's top try scorer with 58 tries in 41 games. He played in Widnes' 6–12 defeat by Wigan in the [[1988–89 League Cup (rugby league)|1988–89 John Player Special Trophy]] final [[Burnden Park]] in [[Bolton]].{{cite web|url=http://wigan.rlfans.com/news.php?readmore=2756|title=7th January 1989: Wigan 12 Widnes 6 (John Player Trophy Final)|publisher=wigan.rlfans.com|date=31 December 2014|access-date=1 January 2015|archive-date=18 May 2015|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20150518082424/http://wigan.rlfans.com/news.php?readmore=2756|url-status=dead}} He then spent the summer in Australia playing for [[Sydney Roosters|Eastern Suburbs]]. He scored a try on his [[NSWRL]] début against [[Western Suburbs Magpies|Western Suburbs]], and went on to score nine tries in 12 games for the club before returning to Widnes. During the [[1989–90 Rugby Football League season|1989–90 season]], Offiah played for Widnes on the wing in their [[1989 World Club Challenge]] victory against the visiting [[Canberra Raiders]]. Offiah scored a try in Widnes' 24–18 victory over [[Salford Red Devils|Salford]] in the [[1990 Lancashire Cup]] final. In 1991, Offiah had a second spell in Australia, this time playing for [[St George Dragons]]. He scored 11 tries in 14 games, including a hat trick against his former club, Eastern Suburbs.{{cite news|last1=Rowlands|first1=David|title='Chariots' Offiah Sinks Easts with a Hat-trick|url=http://newsstore.fairfax.com.au/apps/viewDocument.ac?sy=smh&docID=news910623_0113_9340|work=The Sydney Morning Herald|date=23 June 1991|page=45}} Over four seasons at Widnes Offiah scored 181 tries in 145 games. ===1992–95: Wigan=== '''Inc. 1993 summer stint at Eastern Suburbs''' Offiah joined [[Wigan Warriors|Wigan]] in January 1992 for a world record fee of £440,000{{cite web|url=http://rugby.widnes.tv/playerbiog.php?mid=611|title=Widnes Player Biog|publisher=Rugby.widnes.tv|access-date=2014-07-29}} (based on increases in average earnings, this would be approximately £979,384.20 in 2025).{{cite web|url=http://www.measuringworth.com/ukcompare|title=Measuring Worth – Relative Value of UK Pounds|publisher=Measuring Worth|date=31 December 2014|access-date=1 January 2015}} This remained a record for a total cash transfer until Wigan paid £450,000 for [[Stuart Fielden]] from the [[Bradford Bulls]] in 2006. Playing in his regular left wing position, Offiah formed a devastating partnership with [[Gene Miles]] in his first season at Wigan, scoring 30 tries in just 15 games. He set a new club record of ten in a single match against Leeds in the Premiership semi-final in May.[http://www.wiganwarriors.com/WContent.aspx?id=75 "RECORDS"] {{webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20101128221032/http://www.wiganwarriors.com/WContent.aspx?id=75 |date=28 November 2010}} at ''wiganwarriors.com'' Offiah played in Wigan's 5–4 victory over St Helens in the [[1992 Lancashire Cup]] Final at [[Knowsley Road]].{{cite web|url=http://wigan.rlfans.com/news.php?readmore=2745|title=1988–1989 Lancashire Cup Final|publisher=wigan.rlfans.com|date=31 December 2014|access-date=1 January 2015|archive-date=13 July 2018|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180713170348/http://wigan.rlfans.com/news.php?readmore=2745|url-status=dead}} He played in a 15–8 victory over [[Bradford Bulls|Bradford Northern]] in the [[1992–93 League Cup (rugby league)|1992–93 Regal Trophy]] Final at [[Elland Road]],{{cite web|url=http://wigan.rlfans.com/news.php?readmore=3004|title=23rd January 1993: Bradford 8 Wigan 15 (Regal Trophy Final)|publisher=wigan.rlfans.com|date=31 December 2014|access-date=1 January 2015|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180619140552/http://wigan.rlfans.com/news.php?readmore=3004|archive-date=19 June 2018|url-status=dead}} played in the 2–33 defeat by [[Castleford Tigers|Castleford]] in the [[1993–94 League Cup (rugby league)|1993–94]] Regal Trophy Final at [[Headingley Rugby Stadium|Headingley]], and scored a [[Try (rugby)|try]] in the 40–10 victory over Warrington in the [[1994–95 League Cup (rugby league)|1994–95]] Regal Trophy Final at [[John Smith's Stadium|Alfred McAlpine Stadium]],{{cite web|url=http://wigan.rlfans.com/news.php?readmore=2931|title=28th January 1995: Warrington 10 Wigan 40 (Regal Trophy Final)|publisher=wigan.rlfans.com|date=31 December 2014|access-date=1 January 2015|archive-date=14 July 2014|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20140714151036/http://wigan.rlfans.com/news.php?readmore=2931|url-status=dead}} and played in the 25–16 victory over St Helens in the [[1995–96 League Cup (rugby league)|1995–96]] Regal Trophy Final.{{cite web|url=http://wigan.rlfans.com/news.php?readmore=2976|title=13th January 1996: St Helens 16 Wigan 25 (Regal Trophy Final)|publisher=wigan.rlfans.com|date=31 December 2014|access-date=1 January 2015|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20181007005311/http://wigan.rlfans.com/news.php?readmore=2976|archive-date=7 October 2018|url-status=dead}} He was selected to go on the [[1992 Great Britain Lions tour of Australia and New Zealand]]. Before the match against the [[Parramatta Eels]], Offiah had a 100-metre race against Eels winger [[Lee Oudenryn]]. Offiah appeared to slip slightly at the start and Oudenryn won by half a yard.{{citation needed|date=May 2023}} During the [[1992–93 Rugby Football League season|1992–93 season]] Offiah played on the wing for defending RFL champions Wigan in the [[1992 World Club Challenge]] against the visiting [[Brisbane Broncos]]. A week before the WCC, Offiah had played on the wing for Great Britain in the [[1992 Rugby League World Cup final|World Cup final]] against Australia at [[Wembley Stadium (1923)|Wembley]] in front of an international rugby league record crowd of 73,631 fans.{{cite web|url=http://www.rugbyleagueproject.org/matches/world-cup-1989/final/great-britain-vs-australia.html|title=Great Britain vs. Australia – Rugby League Project|first=Shawn Dollin and Andrew|last=Ferguson}} Offiah played in four [[Challenge Cup]] wins, five [[Rugby Football League Championship|Championship]] teams, three [[League Cup (rugby league)|Regal Trophy]] wins and one [[RFL Lancashire Cup|Lancashire Cup]] victory. He was twice [[player of the match|man of the match]] winning the [[Lance Todd Trophy]] at Wembley. His 90-metre solo try in the 1994 Challenge Cup Final win over Leeds is remembered as one of the finest ever seen in a final,{{cite web|url=https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=mBtoUy5cHYU |archive-url=https://ghostarchive.org/varchive/youtube/20211213/mBtoUy5cHYU |archive-date=2021-12-13 |url-status=live|title=Martin Offiah Wembley 1994|last=Wigan Rugby League Old Matches|date=3 May 2010|via=YouTube}}{{cbignore}} but he made a habit of such runs during his career, scoring a similar try just weeks before against Castleford. After the [[1993–94 Rugby Football League season|1993–94 season]] Offiah travelled with defending champions [[Wigan Warriors|Wigan]] to Brisbane, playing on the wing in their [[1994 World Club Challenge]] victory over Australian premiers, the [[Brisbane Broncos]]. In 1995, Offiah's life story, a 30-minute documentary, was produced and directed by [[Pogus Caesar]] for [[Carlton Television|Carlton TV]] UK as part of the ''[[Respect (TV series)|Respect]]'' series. Offiah was selected to play for [[England national rugby league team|England]] in the [[1995 Rugby League World Cup|1995 World Cup]] final on the wing, but [[Australia national rugby league team|Australia]] won the match and retained the cup. Offiah jointly holds the record for the most [[Try (rugby)|tries]] scored in a match for England.{{cn|date=October 2025}} Offiah made 158 appearances for Wigan with another single appearance as a substitute. He scored 186 tries, more than a try a game. He also managed a goal and three drop goals, scoring a total of 749 points for the club. Upon leaving he sent a letter to the ''[[Wigan Observer]]'' thanking fans and the club for his time there.{{cn|date=October 2025}} ===1996–99: London Broncos=== '''Inc. 1996–97 winter stint at Bedford Blues''' Following Offiah's departure from Wigan, he return to the capital and signed for [[London Broncos]]. Following the switch to summer rugby in 1996, Offiah signed for [[Bedford Blues]] during the Super League off-season He played 14 games for the club, scoring 10 tries, before returning to the Broncos for the 1997 season. He was awarded an [[Order of the British Empire|MBE]] in the [[New Year Honours 1997|1997 New Year Honours]].{{London Gazette |issue=54625 |date=30 December 1996 |page=21}} He played for London in their [[1999 Challenge Cup]] Final defeat against Leeds. In total, he scored 24 tries in 48 games for the club. ===2000–01: Salford Red Devils=== After the London Broncos he moved to [[Salford City Reds]] for the 2000 [[Super League V]], staying for two seasons, playing 45 games and scoring 23 tries. He scored his 500th career try in July 2001 in a 37–14 win against his former club London Broncos.{{cite web |title=Overjoyed Offiah drops quit hint |url=https://news.bbc.co.uk/sport2/hi/rugby_league/1417203.stm |publisher=BBC Sport |access-date=21 March 2023 |date=1 July 2001}} ===2001–02: Wasps=== He finished his career in 2001–02 playing [[rugby union]] for [[Wasps RFC|London Wasps]], who were coached by his former Wigan team-mate [[Shaun Edwards]]. He played in eight games for the club, scoring five tries. ==Career statistics== ===Club=== {| class="wikitable" style="text-align:center" |+ Appearance and scoring statistics by club and season |- || 1 || 5 || 1,791 |} ===Overall=== Offiah scored 501 tries during his rugby league career. His try total in British rugby league (481) puts him third in the list of all-time try scorers (behind [[Brian Bevan]] and [[Billy Boston]]).{{cite web |title=Offiah to retire from league New Year Honours]] ==Post-playing career== In 2004, Offiah was one of the celebrities who took part in the first series of the [[BBC One]] show ''[[Strictly Come Dancing]]'', reaching fourth place with his partner [[Erin Boag]]. In 2005, alongside [[Natasha Kaplinsky]], he hosted the one-off special, ''Strictly African Dancing''. In 2006, he appeared on a Reality TV special of ''[[The Weakest Link (British game show)|The Weakest Link]]'', winning £10,050 and splitting the winnings with [[Danniella Westbrook]]. Offiah won Celebrity Come Dine With Me in 2011 which was a Rugby World Cup special. Offiah beat Victor Ubogo, Kyran Bracken and Gareth Chilcott. Offiah has also appeared on BBC quiz show ''[[Pointless]]'', firstly with [[Chris Kamara]] in 2012, where they were eliminated in the first round, the second time in 2013 with [[cricket]]er [[Phil Tufnell|Philip Tufnell]] where they won the cash prize by getting a Pointless answer in the final round. Offiah donated his £1,250 prize to the Joining Jack Charity. In 2014, he was a contestant on ''[[Splash! (British TV series)|Splash!]]'', and was the fourth to be eliminated from the competition. In 2015, he appeared with his son Tyler on ''[[Big Star's Little Star]]'' where they won £11,000 for charity. Also in 2015, Offiah took part on ''[[All Star Mr & Mrs]]'' with his partner Virginia. Offiah worked as a pundit for [[Sky Sports]] rugby from 2008 until 2013. He was a regular studio guest during the [[2008 Rugby League World Cup]], and presented a weekly [[Super League]] round up on [[Sky Sports News]]. He has worked as an agent for several rugby players including [[Richie Myler]] and [[Kyle Eastmond]]. Offiah has also appeared several times on the TV show ''[[A Question of Sport]]''. In January 2018, Offiah participated in [[And They're Off!]] in aid of [[Sport Relief]]. Offiah is part of the Rugby League Monument that sits outside Wembley Stadium, his bronze likeness depicting his try celebration from the 1994 Challenge Cup final. Since 2019, Offiah has been a brand ambassador for the electric vehicle charging infrastructure company Connected Kerb.{{cite web|title=Rugby player Martin Offiah joins Connected Kerb {{!}} Connected Kerb|url=https://www.connectedkerb.com/stories/martin_offiah_joins_connected_kerb|access-date=1 January 2022|website=connectedkerb.com}} His passion for electric vehicles began when he received a lift from former England rugby union player and World Cup winner [[Andy Gomarsall|Andy Gomersal]] in 2016.{{cite web|last=Smith|first=Mark|date=16 June 2021|title=Widnes Rugby icon Martin Offiah launches new green vehicle scheme|url=https://www.liverpoolecho.co.uk/news/liverpool-news/widnes-rugby-legend-strictly-come-20828673|access-date=1 January 2022|website=Liverpool Echo}} In December 2021, he facilitated a partnership between Connected Kerb and one of his former clubs [[London Broncos]] in which the [[Plough Lane]] outfit would wear the EV company's logo on their kit throughout 2022.{{cite web|last=Media|first=Broncos|date=16 December 2021|title=Connected Kerb get behind Broncos for 2022 with familiar face involved in partnership.|url=https://londonbroncosrl.com/2021/12/16/connected-kerb-get-behind-broncos-for-2022-with-familiar-face-involved-in-partnership/|access-date=1 January 2022|website=London Broncos RL}} Since 2024, Offiah has trained grappling at Grange Jiu Jitsu, and was awarded his Brazilian Jiu Jitsu blue belt in 2025. ==Personal life== Offiah has two sons, including [[Tyler Offiah|Tyler]] with his wife, Virginia Offiah, and lives in [[Ealing]], [[West London]].{{cite web |last=McGreevy |first=Hannah |date=12 February 2022 |title='Your history is your history' Rugby legend Martin Offiah weighs in on royal family debate |url=https://www.express.co.uk/celebrity-news/1563919/martin-offiah-royal-family-news-debate-rugby-latest-update |access-date=1 March 2022 |website=Express.co.uk}} He is the uncle of professional footballer [[Odel Offiah]].{{cite news |url= https://www.express.co.uk/sport/football/1113968/Brighton-transfer-news-Odell-Offiah-Chris-Hughton-Bournemouth |title=Brighton
